narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown real determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which stands out above the typical student's average. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 59% chance that you might not pass it right away—and that's why your application was rejected this time. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of around 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school raised concerns about bar exam readiness. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA pushed strongly in the opposite direction, toward a prediction of passing, as it exceeded what most applicants bring. Your LSAT score of 27, lower than the typical 36.7, also contributed toward the failure prediction, adding to the model's worries despite your other merits. Meanwhile, your Year 1 decile of 3 offered some helpful counterbalance toward passing, your middle family income bracket nudged slightly toward failure compared to higher averages, and your plan to attend full-time aligned with the vast majority of successful students, mildly supporting a pass. Overall, compared to typical students who pass, your profile had key gaps in later school grades and LSAT that outweighed strengths like your GPA, tipping the scales. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just one model's data-driven view. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, seeking ways to improve your academic decile trajectory through targeted prep or advanced courses, and highlighting any bar exam-focused experiences. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these areas head-on. Keep pushing forward; your story is far from over, and law schools value resilient applicants like you. We're rooting for your next steps.
